title: Calculating the P-value in a z test for a proportion
description: Given sample results and the test statistic, practice calculating the P-value in a one-sample z test for a proportion.
topic_path: Calculating the P-value in a z test for a proportion

assessment_items.question_content:
A public opinion survey investigated whether a majority of adults supported a tax increase to help fund the local school system. A random sample of $200$ adults showed that $120$ of those sampled supported the tax increase. Researchers want to use these results to test $H_0:p=0.5$ versus $H_\text{a} : p>0.5$, where $p$ is the true proportion of adults that support the tax increase.

**Which of the following represents the P-value for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###What is a P-value?

We can think of a P-value as a conditional probability: given the null hypothesis is true, what's the probability of obtaining a sample statistic as extreme or more than the one observed by random chance alone. In this type of test, we use the alternative hypothesis $H_\text{a}$ to decide if the P-value comes from the probability above the test statistic, below the test statistic, or comes from a two-sided probability.

###Formula for test statistic $z$

We can calculate the test statistic corresponding to the sample result:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{statistic}-\text{parameter}}{\text{standard deviation of statistic}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}}
\end{align}$

(where $\hat p$ is the sample proportion, $p_0$ is the proportion from the null hypothesis, and $n$ is the sample size).

###Direction of test

Since the alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a} : p>0.5$, our P-value is the probability of obtaining a sample proportion *greater than* $\hat p=\dfrac{120}{200}=0.6.$

$\text{P-value}=P\left( z>\dfrac{0.6-0.5}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.5(0.5)}{200}}} \right)$

###Answer

The P-value for their test is:

$P\left( z>\dfrac{0.6-0.5}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.5(0.5)}{200}}} \right)$

assessment_items.question_content:
A campaign manager for a political candidate released a series of advertisements criticizing the opposing candidate in an upcoming election. The opposing candidate previously had the support of $45\%$ of voters, so the manager wants to test $H_0:p=0.45$ versus $H_\text{a}:p < 0.45$, where $p$ is the proportion of voters that support the opposing candidate.

After running the advertisements, the campaign manager obtained a random sample of $500$ voters and found that $200$ of those sampled supported the opposing candidate.

**Which of the following represents the P-value for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###What is a P-value?

We can think of a P-value as a conditional probability: given the null hypothesis is true, what's the probability of obtaining a sample statistic as extreme or more than the one observed by random chance alone. In this type of test, we use the alternative hypothesis $H_\text{a}$ to decide if the P-value comes from the probability above the test statistic, below the test statistic, or comes from a two-sided probability.

###Formula for test statistic $z$

We can calculate the test statistic corresponding to the sample result:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{statistic}-\text{parameter}}{\text{standard deviation of statistic}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}}
\end{align}$

(where $\hat p$ is the sample proportion, $p_0$ is the proportion from the null hypothesis, and $n$ is the sample size).

###Direction of test

Since the alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a}:p < 0.45$, our P-value is the probability of obtaining a sample proportion *less than* $\hat p=\dfrac{200}{500}=0.4.$

$\text{P-value}=P\left( z<\dfrac{0.4-0.45}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.45(0.55)}{500}}} \right)$

###Answer

The P-value for their test is:

$P\left( z<\dfrac{0.4-0.45}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.45(0.55)}{500}}} \right)$

assessment_items.question_content:
A business knows that $30\%$ of its customers are less than $25$ years old. The business wants to increase this percentage, so they create a marketing campaign that targets this age group. They want to test $H_0:p=0.3$ versus $H_\text{a}:p > 0.3$, where $p$ is the proportion of this business's customers who are less than $25$ years old after the marketing campaign.

After the campaign, the business obtained a random sample of $50$ customers and found that $18$ of those sampled were less than $25$ years old.

**Which of the following represents the P-value for their test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###What is a P-value?

We can think of a P-value as a conditional probability: given the null hypothesis is true, what's the probability of obtaining a sample statistic as extreme or more than the one observed by random chance alone. In this type of test, we use the alternative hypothesis $H_\text{a}$ to decide if the P-value comes from the probability above the test statistic, below the test statistic, or comes from a two-sided probability.

###Formula for test statistic $z$

We can calculate the test statistic corresponding to the sample result:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{statistic}-\text{parameter}}{\text{standard deviation of statistic}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}}
\end{align}$

(where $\hat p$ is the sample proportion, $p_0$ is the proportion from the null hypothesis, and $n$ is the sample size).

###Direction of test

Since the alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a} : p>0.3$, our P-value is the probability of obtaining a sample proportion *greater than* $\hat p=\dfrac{18}{50}=0.36.$

$\text{P-value}=P\left( z>\dfrac{0.36-0.3}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.3(0.7)}{50}}} \right)$

###Answer

The P-value for their test is:

$P\left( z>\dfrac{0.36-0.3}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.3(0.7)}{50}}} \right)$

assessment_items.question_content:
Aurelia has a coin that she suspects is unfair. She wants to test $H_0:p=0.5$ versus $H_\text{a}:p \neq 0.5$, where $p$ is the proportion of flips that this coin lands showing heads.

She flipped the coin $100$ times and it landed showing heads $43$ of those times.

**Which of the following represents the P-value for Aurelia's test?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###What is a P-value?

We can think of a P-value as a conditional probability: given the null hypothesis is true, what's the probability of obtaining a sample statistic as extreme or more than the one observed by random chance alone. In this type of test, we use the alternative hypothesis $H_\text{a}$ to decide if the P-value comes from the probability above the test statistic, below the test statistic, or comes from a two-sided probability.

###Formula for test statistic $z$

We can calculate the test statistic corresponding to the sample result:

$\begin{align}
z&=\dfrac{\text{statistic}-\text{parameter}}{\text{standard deviation of statistic}} \\\\
&=\dfrac{\hat p -p_0}{\sqrt{\dfrac{p_0 \left( 1-p_0 \right)}{n}}}
\end{align}$

(where $\hat p$ is the sample proportion, $p_0$ is the proportion from the null hypothesis, and $n$ is the sample size).

###Direction of test

Since the alternative hypothesis is $H_\text{a} : p\neq0.5$, this is two-sided test. Our P-value is the probability of obtaining a sample proportion less than $\hat p=\dfrac{43}{100}=0.43$ or greater than $\hat p=\dfrac{57}{100}=0.57$.

![A normal distribution curve has a mean of 50%. Values are marked under the left and right tails at 43% and 57%. The areas under the curve to the left of 43% and to the right of 57% are shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ae6a052a12851f71ef46816df61f83f4ac2c04b6)

We can multiply the lower tail probability by $2$ to calculate the P-value:

$2 \cdot P\left( z<\dfrac{0.43-0.5}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.5(0.5)}{100}}} \right)$

###Answer

The P-value for Aurelia's test is:

$2 \cdot P\left( z<\dfrac{0.43-0.5}{\sqrt{\dfrac{0.5(0.5)}{100}}} \right)$

assessment_items.question_content:
Fay read an article that said $26\%$ of Americans can speak more than one language. She was curious if this figure was higher in her city, so she tested $H_0:p=0.26$ vs. $H_\text{a}:p>0.26$, where $p$ represents the proportion of people in her city that can speak more than one language.

She found that $40$ of $120$ people sampled could speak more than one language. The test statistic for these results was $z\approx1.83$.

**Assuming that the necessary conditions are met, what is the approximate P-value for Fay's test?**  
*You may round to three decimal places.*

P-value $\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
Fay is testing if the proportion of people in her city that can speak more than one language is *higher* than $26\%$ $(H_\text{a}:p>0.26)$, so this is a one-sided test. Our P-value is the area *above* her test statistic $z \approx 1.83$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. mu sub z = 0 and sigma sub z = 1. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at z = 1.83.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4df129c9cfbce1abd4b297a9e85a44ea3a6a6a53)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z>1.83$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 1.83 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.0336
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

P-value $\approx 0.0336$

assessment_items.question_content:
A ketchup company regularly receives large shipments of tomatoes. For quality control purposes, they take a sample of tomatoes from each shipment. If the sample shows convincing evidence that more than $8\%$ of the tomatoes in the entire shipment are bruised, then the company will request a new shipment of tomatoes. So the company tests $H_0: p=0.08$ versus $H_\text{a}:p>0.08$, where $p$ is the proportion of tomatoes in the entire shipment that are bruised.

One day, a supervisor takes a random sample of $600$ tomatoes from a shipment and finds that $53$ of the tomatoes are bruised, which results in a test statistic of $z\approx0.75$.

**Assuming that the necessary conditions are met, what is the approximate P-value associated with the significance test for this shipment?**  
*You may round to three decimal places.*

P-value $\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The researchers are testing if significantly *more* than $8\%$ of the tomatoes are bruised $(H_\text{a}:p>0.08)$, so this is a one-sided test. Our P-value is the area *above* their test statistic $z\approx0.75$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. mu sub z = 0 and sigma sub z = 1. A test statistic is marked before the right tail at z = 0.75.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b9af47e38f13984b44977fe3c93039643a24d6ba)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z>0.75$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 0.75 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\\\
&\text{p-value}\approx0.2266
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

P-value $\approx 0.2266$

assessment_items.question_content:
In 2011, $51\%$ of cell phone owners in a country reported that their cell phone was a smartphone. The following year, the researchers wanted to test $H_0: p=0.51$ versus $H_\text{a}:p>0.51$, where $p$ is the proportion of cell phone owners in that country who have a smartphone.

They surveyed a random sample of $934$ cell phone owners in that country and found that $501$ of them had a smartphone. The test statistic for these results was $z\approx 1.61$.

**Assuming that the necessary conditions are met, what is the approximate P-value for their significance test?**  
*You may round to three decimal places.*

P-value $\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The researchers are testing $H_\text{a}:p>0.51$, so this is a one-sided test. Our p-value is the area *above* their test statistic $z \approx 1.61$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. mu sub z = 0 and sigma sub z = 1. A test statistic is marked under the right tail at z = 1.61.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/09f24c046e13d98faaaba9f0975d541d7592eec5)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z>1.61$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 1.61 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 999 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\\\
&\text{p-value} \approx 0.0537
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

P-value $\approx 0.0537$

assessment_items.question_content:
According to a large poll in a previous year, about $80\%$ of homes in a certain county had access to high-speed internet. The following year, researchers wanted to test $H_0: p=0.8$ versus $H_\text{a}:p<0.8$, where $p$ is the proportion of homes in this county with high-speed internet access.

They took a random sample of $250$ homes from that county and found that $189$ of them had access to high-speed internet. The test statistic for these results was $z\approx -1.74$.

**Assuming that the necessary conditions are met, what is the approximate P-value for this significance test?**  
*You may round to three decimal places.*

P-value $\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
They are testing $H_\text{a}:p<0.8$, so this is a one-sided test. Our P-value is the area *below* their test statistic $z \approx -1.74$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. mu sub z = 0 and sigma sub z = 1. A test statistic is marked under the left tail at z = negative 1.74.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/585defda52b850b06a2ead13e08a724542b8a2ab)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z<-1.74$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } -999 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } -1.74 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.0409
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

P-value $\approx 0.0409$

assessment_items.question_content:
The mayor of a town read an article that claimed the national unemployment rate was $8\%$. They suspected that the unemployment rate was lower in their town, so they took a sample of $128$ residents to test $H_0: p=0.08$ versus $H_\text{a}:p<0.08$, where $p$ is the proportion of residents that are unemployed.

They found that $6$ residents in the sample were unemployed, and the corresponding test statistic was $z \approx -1.38$.

**Assuming that the necessary conditions are met, what is the approximate P-value for this significance test?**  
*You may round to three decimal places.*

P-value $\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
The mayor is testing if the unemployment rate in their town is *lower* than $8\%$ $(H_\text{a}:p<0.08)$, so this is a one-sided test. Our P-value is the area *below* their test statistic $z\approx -1.38$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. mu sub z = 0 and sigma sub z = 1. A test statistic is marked under the left tail at z = negative 1.38.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a8f8e618f8f8e4d16b6be761a14fd75fa8df408d)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z<-1.38$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } -999 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } -1.38\\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.0838
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

P-value $\approx 0.0838$

assessment_items.question_content:
Abdullah is a quality control expert at a factory that paints car parts. He knew that the factory was painting $20\%$ of the parts with an error, so he made a change in the painting process. After the change, he wanted to test $H_0:p=0.2$ versus $H_\text{a}:p < 0.2$, where $p$ is the proportion of parts that the factory was painting with an error.

Abdullah took a sample of $400$ parts and found that $70$ had an error, and the corresponding test statistic was $z=-1.25$.

**Assuming that the necessary conditions are met, what is the approximate P-value for Abdullah's significance test?**  
*You may round to three decimal places.*

P-value $\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
Abdullah is testing $H_\text{a}:p<0.2$, so this is a one-sided test. Our P-value is the area *below* his test statistic $z=-1.25$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. mu sub z = 0 and sigma sub z = 1. A test statistic is marked under the left tail at z = negative 1.25.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/27faba4b19397e44c6a5eaf9a91e343da9138e36)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z<-1.25$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } -999 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } -1.25\\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\\\
&\text{P-value} \approx 0.1056
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

P-value $\approx 0.1056$

assessment_items.question_content:
Amanda read a report saying that $49\%$ of teachers in the United States were members of a labor union. She wanted to test whether this was true in her state, so she took a random sample of $300$ teachers from her state to test $H_0:p=0.49$ versus $H_\text{a}:p \neq 0.49$, where $p$ is the proportion of teachers in her state who are members of a labor union.

The sample results showed $134$ teachers were members of a labor union, and the corresponding test statistic was $z\approx-1.50$.

**Assuming that the necessary conditions are met, what is the approximate P-value for Amanda's significance test?**  
*You may round to three decimal places.*

P-value $\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
Amanda is testing *if it's true* that the proportion of teachers in her state who are members of a labor union is $49\%$ $(H_\text{a}:p \neq 0.49)$, so this is a two-sided test. Our P-value is the area *below* her test statistic $z\approx -1.50$ combined with the area *above* $z \approx 1.50$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. mu sub z = 0 and sigma sub z = 1. Test statistics are marked under the left and right tails at z = negative 1.50 and z = 1.50.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4b882c245924f564fa1e204566f7546a2ed6fdae)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z<-1.50$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } -999 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } -1.50 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\\\
&\text{output} \approx 0.0668
\end{align}$

We can multiply that output by $2$ to get the combined area in both tails:

$\begin{align}
\text{P-value} &\approx 0.0668 \times 2 \text{ tails} \\\\
&\approx 0.1336
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

###Answer

P-value $\approx 0.1336$

assessment_items.question_content:
Elliot read a report from a previous year saying that $6\%$ of adults in his city biked to work. He wanted to test whether this had changed, so he took a random sample of $240$ adults in his city to test $H_0:p=0.06$ versus $H_\text{a}:p \neq 0.06$, where $p$ is the proportion of adults in Elliot's city that bike to work.

The sample results showed $21$ adults who biked to work, and the corresponding test statistic was $z \approx 1.79$.

**Assuming that the necessary conditions are met, what is the approximate P-value for Elliot's significance test?**  
*You may round to three decimal places.*

P-value $\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
Elliot is testing if the proportion of adults in his city who bike to work *has changed* from $6\%$ $(H_\text{a}:p \neq 0.06)$, so this is a two-sided test. Our P-value is the area *above* his test statistic $z\approx 1.79$ combined with the area *below* $z \approx -1.79$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. mu sub z = 0 and sigma sub z = 1. Test statistics are marked under the left and right tails at z = negative 1.79 and z = 1.79.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0843679b32cac60f6c7959d4335a749d8bfbf09e)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z<-1.79$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } -999 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } -1.79 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\\\
&\text{output} \approx 0.0367
\end{align}$

We can multiply that output by $2$ to get the combined area in both tails:

$\begin{align}
\text{P-value} &\approx 0.0367 \times 2 \text{ tails} \\\\
&\approx 0.0734
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 2]]

###Answer

P-value $\approx 0.0734$

assessment_items.question_content:
Ellie read an article claiming that $10\%$ of people in her county were senior citizens, and she wondered if this held true for residents in her city. She took a random sample of $225$ people from her city to test $H_0:p=0.10$ versus $H_\text{a}:p \neq 0.10$, where $p$ is the proportion of people in her city that are senior citizens.

She found that $31$ of those sampled were senior citizens, and the corresponding test statistic was $z \approx 1.89$.

**Assuming that the necessary conditions are met, what is the approximate P-value for Ellie's significance test?**  
*You may round to three decimal places.*

P-value $\approx$ [[☃ numeric-input 1]]
hints:
Ellie is testing *if it's true* that the proportion of people in her city who are senior citizens is $10\%$ $(H_\text{a}:p \neq 0.10)$, so this is a two-sided test. Our P-value is the area *above* her test statistic $z\approx 1.89$ combined with the area *below* $z \approx -1.89$ in a standard normal distribution:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on a z axis. mu sub z = 0 and sigma sub z = 1. Test statistics are marked under the left and right tails at z = negative 1.89 and z = 1.89.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4cf241aae07b634b49899f920039fbec00f8926d)

We can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $z<-1.89$:

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } -999 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } -1.89 \\\\
&\mu=0 \\\\
&\sigma=1 \\\\\\
&\text{output} \approx 0.0294
\end{align}$

We can multiply that output by $2$ to get the combined area in both tails:

$\begin{align}
\text{P-value} &\approx 0.0294 \times 2 \text{ tails} \\\\
&\approx 0.0588
\end{align}$

[[☃ explanation 3]]

###Answer

P-value $\approx 0.0588$
